one_line_takeaway: By exploiting the discovered link that an infected model's adversarial examples resemble its triggered images, PUD unifies backdoor erasing and adversarial-robustness training into one iterative purification loop that works even when the defender's extra data is itself poisoned.
key_finding: PUD reduces backdoor attack success rate to near-zero while retaining clean accuracy, and does so WITHOUT a clean extra dataset, outperforming its predecessor PBE and SoTA repairing/filtering defenses across attacks. On CIFAR-10 all-to-one with a 10% poisoned (non-clean) extra set, PUD cuts SIG ASR to 0.31% (vs PBE 3.53%), Blend to 0.55% (vs PBE 1.15%), BadNet to 1.44% (vs PBE 3.41%), at ~92-94% ACC, versus ~99-100% ASR before defense. It simultaneously delivers competitive adversarial robustness: CIFAR-10 RACC 76.13% (AT 77.49%, TRADES 71.07%) and sub-ImgNet1K RACC 79.89%, edging out both AT (79.18%) and TRADES (77.75%). The observation itself is shown to be ubiquitous across attacks, datasets and attack settings (Fig.2/6, feature-distance Tables 1-2).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Progressive Unified Defense (PUD), a model-repairing defense that erases DNN backdoors and improves adversarial robustness simultaneously. The conceptual foundation - that for an infected model, its untargeted adversarial examples are highly likely to be classified as the backdoor target-label and have feature maps close to the triggered images - was established in the authors' OWN prior work PBE (Mu et al., CVPR 2023, ref [36]); this paper carries that connection forward and adds a theoretical justification for a linear/logistic-regression model (Theorem 1, bounding the projection of the adversarial perturbation onto the trigger direction). The genuinely NEW contribution over PBE is fourfold: (i) a teacher-student mean-teacher (EMA weight-averaging) mechanism that boosts both backdoor erasing and adversarial robustness; (ii) combining the prediction-consistency data-purification strategy with the SPECTRE data-filtering method (shown to be complementary); (iii) a machine-unlearning step (gradient-ascent with an over-unlearning regularizer) that reuses the identified poisoned images for further backdoor erasing; and (iv) extending the defense scope from backdoor-only (PBE) to jointly covering inference-time adversarial attacks. PUD also claims to be the first backdoor defense that does not require a completely clean extra dataset.
threat_model: Training-time backdoor threat plus inference-time adversarial threat, unified. The adversary controls the training data and plants a backdoor (all-to-one or all-to-all target labeling) via a static trigger (BadNets patch, Blend, SIG) or a dynamic/content-aware trigger (input-aware DyAtt, warping-based WaNet); the infected model is then handed to the defender. Two defense settings are considered: model-repairing (defender has only the infected model plus a small extra dataset, performs defense after training - the paper's main focus) and data-filtering (defender can access all poisoned training data during training). A key relaxation of the standard threat model is that the defender's extra dataset need NOT be clean - it may itself contain poisoned images, which PUD purifies by itself. Separately, the model must also withstand inference-time PGD/C&W/l-inf adversarial perturbations. Note: the threat model assumes a non-adaptive attacker unaware of the PUD defense.
limitations: Paper-stated / table-evident: (1) PUD is slightly weaker than Neural Cleanse against BadNet (a fixed-trigger attack) - e.g. on CIFAR-10 all-to-one NC reaches 0.83% ASR vs PUD 1.44% - because BadNet needs very few poison samples and PUD cannot 100% filter them out of the extra set; (2) adversarial robustness is only 'competitive,' not dominant - on CIFAR-10 PUD RACC 76.13% is below AT's 77.49%, and on GTSRB PUD RACC 68.46% is below both AT (70.77%) and TRADES (74.01%); (3) an ablation (Table 8) shows a tension: SPECTRE/mean-teacher raise RACC but can worsen ASR, while unlearning lowers ASR - the components trade off. Reviewer-inferred (not paper admissions): the threat model excludes adaptive attackers who know PUD is deployed; evaluation is small-scale (CIFAR-10, GTSRB, and only 10 of 1000 ImageNet classes) on PreAct-ResNet18/ResNet18; the adversarial-defense comparison uses only PGD; and the theoretical guarantee (Theorem 1) is derived only for a linear logistic-regression model, not the deep CNNs actually used.

datasets_used: sub-ImageNet-1K (10 randomly selected of 1000 classes, ResNet18, 224x224)
quantitative_results: PUD without clean extra data, CIFAR-10 all-to-one: SIG ASR 0.31% (vs PBE 3.53%, vs 98.96% before defense), at 92.48% ACC
PUD without clean extra data, CIFAR-10 all-to-one: Blend ASR 0.55% (vs PBE 1.15%), BadNet ASR 1.44% (vs PBE 3.41%), DyAtt 0.53%, WaNet 0.76%
Neural Cleanse edge case, CIFAR-10 all-to-one BadNet: NC ASR 0.83% beats PUD 1.44%; but NC FAILS (F) on dynamic DyAtt/WaNet where PUD stays <0.8% ASR
Adversarial robustness, CIFAR-10 (PGD): PUD RACC 76.13% / ACC 92.32% vs AT 77.49%/89.46% vs TRADES 71.07%/84.81% vs Normal 0.01%/94.01%
Adversarial robustness, GTSRB (PGD): PUD RACC 68.46% below AT 70.77% and TRADES 74.01%; ACC PUD 98.45%
Adversarial robustness, sub-ImgNet1K (PGD): PUD RACC 79.89% beats AT 79.18% and TRADES 77.75%
Data-filtering, poisoned-image recall on CIFAR-10 with 5000 poisoned images: PUD filters BadNet 4992/5000, SIG 5000/5000, Blind 5000/5000, AdvDoor 4988/5000 vs SPECTRE 4259/2740 and Spectral ~3465

Data purification with 500 poisoned images, CIFAR-10 Blend: PUD 500/500 vs SPECTRE 492/500 vs prediction-only/PBE 483/500 (SPECTRE degrades as poison count rises, prediction-based stays stable - hence complementary)
Ablation (Blend, CIFAR-10): PUD ACC 92.01% / ASR 0.55% / RACC 59.35%; PBE alone 90.88/1.15/50.64; +mean-teacher raises RACC to 56.74; +SPECTRE to 61.13; +unlearning drops ASR to 0.48

Feature similarity (l2 distance), CIFAR-10: distance of infected-model adv-example to triggered image (Dtrigger) far smaller than to benign adv-example (Dbenign), e.g. BadNet 0.97 vs 2.09, WaNet 0.90 vs 2.64
baselines_compared: External model-repairing defenses: Fine-tuning, Fine-pruning, Neural Cleanse (NC), NAD, ANP, I-BAU
External data-filtering defenses: Spectral Signatures, SPECTRE
External adversarial defenses: Adversarial Training (AT), TRADES
Authors' own methods used for comparison/ablation (NOT external baselines): PBE (their prior CVPR 2023 work that PUD extends) and AFT (PUD's own single-pass clean-data simplification)
